metadata.title: Prescriptions Outside NZ
metadata.description: Medicines commonly prescribed overseas that are unavailable, unfunded, or differently restricted in NZ. For consultation support only - always verify current PHARMAC status at pharmac.govt.nz and NZF status at nzf.org.nz before prescribing.
metadata.note: Confidence ratings reflect the author's certainty about NZ status at time of writing, not clinical evidence quality. Funding decisions change - verify before acting.

entries.originStatus: Registered and available in Australia (Duromine) for short-term weight loss. Widely prescribed in the US.
entries.nzStatusNote: Phentermine is not registered with Medsafe and cannot be legally prescribed or dispensed in NZ.
entries.nzEquivalent: Orlistat (Xenical) - funded via Special Authority for BMI ≥30 (or ≥27 with comorbidity) where lifestyle measures have failed. Bariatric referral pathway available.

entries.originStatus: One of the most commonly prescribed medications in the US for anxiety and panic disorder. Available in Australia but not PBS-funded.
entries.nzStatusNote: Alprazolam (Xanax) is not registered with Medsafe and is not available in NZ. Patients sometimes bring supplies from overseas.
entries.nzEquivalent: Diazepam (funded) or clonazepam (funded) for anxiety/panic. Lorazepam available but less commonly used long-term. If transitioning, note that direct alprazolam-to-diazepam conversion is not straightforward - consider dose equivalence carefully.

entries.originStatus: First-line ADHD treatment in the US. Available as immediate-release and extended-release. Not available in most other countries.
entries.nzStatusNote: Mixed amphetamine salts are not a registered product in NZ. Patients arriving from the US with Adderall prescriptions cannot have them dispensed here.
entries.nzEquivalent: Methylphenidate (Ritalin, Rubifen) - funded via Special Authority, psychiatrist or paediatrician initiation required. Dexamfetamine (Dexedrine) - available via SA. Lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) - funded via SA. Atomoxetine (Strattera) - non-stimulant alternative, funded via SA.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for neuropathic pain (post-herpetic neuralgia, diabetic peripheral neuropathy) and epilepsy. Widely used in the UK for neuropathic pain on NHS.
entries.nzStatusNote: Pregabalin is funded in NZ via Special Authority for epilepsy only. It is NOT funded for neuropathic pain. Available private but costly.
entries.nzEquivalent: Gabapentin (Neurontin) - funded for neuropathic pain. Amitriptyline, nortriptyline - funded, often first-line in NZ primary care for neuropathic pain. Duloxetine - funded for diabetic peripheral neuropathy via SA.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for ED. Available OTC in some countries. Widely prescribed under NHS in UK for ED.
entries.nzStatusNote: Sildenafil is funded in NZ only for pulmonary arterial hypertension (via SA, specialist-initiated). For erectile dysfunction, it is available by prescription but at full private cost. Tadalafil is similarly unfunded for ED.
entries.nzEquivalent: No funded equivalent for ED in NZ. Private prescription required. Generic sildenafil is available and relatively affordable privately.

entries.originStatus: Available over the counter in Australia (2 mg+), the US, and many other countries. In the UK, Circadin is prescription-only but widely used.
entries.nzStatusNote: Melatonin is prescription-only in NZ regardless of dose. Circadin 2 mg (prolonged-release) is funded via Special Authority for primary insomnia in patients aged 55 and over. Not funded for other indications or younger patients. Patients sometimes self-import or bring OTC supplies from Australia.
entries.nzEquivalent: Circadin 2 mg (funded via SA, age ≥55, primary insomnia). For younger patients or other indications: private prescription required.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for hypercholesterolaemia in combination with statins. Widely used on NHS in the UK.
entries.nzStatusNote: Ezetimibe is funded in NZ via Special Authority for patients with established CVD who cannot tolerate adequate statin doses, or for familial hypercholesterolaemia. Criteria are stricter than PBS.
entries.nzEquivalent: Maximise statin therapy first. Rosuvastatin (funded, up to 40 mg) and atorvastatin (funded) should be optimised before seeking SA for ezetimibe.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for T2DM and heart failure. Widely used in UK on NHS with relatively broad criteria. Standard of care in most guidelines.
entries.nzStatusNote: Both empagliflozin and dapagliflozin are funded in NZ via Special Authority. Criteria include T2DM with established cardiovascular disease, or high cardiovascular/renal risk meeting specific thresholds. Heart failure indications also available. Criteria have been broadening - check current PHARMAC schedule.
entries.nzEquivalent: Same drugs, but SA required. Initiation typically in primary care once SA criteria are met. Verify current criteria at pharmac.govt.nz as these have been updated.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for T2DM. Widely used internationally. Supply shortages have occurred globally.
entries.nzStatusNote: Semaglutide (Ozempic) is funded in NZ via Special Authority for T2DM, requiring inadequate control on metformin plus another agent, with specific HbA1c and BMI or CVD criteria. For weight loss (Wegovy), semaglutide is not funded in NZ as of writing.
entries.nzEquivalent: For weight loss: no funded GLP-1 agonist. For T2DM: meet SA criteria. Liraglutide (Victoza) has similar but differently worded SA criteria.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia for chronic weight management. Funded on NHS in UK for obesity with comorbidities. Standard weight management agent in many countries.
entries.nzStatusNote: Wegovy (semaglutide 2.4 mg) is not funded in NZ for weight management. It is available on private prescription. Ozempic (semaglutide for T2DM) is funded via SA for diabetes only - prescribing it off-label for weight loss is not funded and raises ethical/supply concerns.
entries.nzEquivalent: Orlistat (funded via SA). Bariatric surgery referral. Ozempic if T2DM criteria met.

entries.originStatus: PBS-funded in Australia with relatively broad criteria for AF, VTE treatment and prevention. Standard of care widely funded internationally.
entries.nzStatusNote: All three DOACs are funded in NZ via Special Authority for atrial fibrillation (non-valvular, with risk factors) and VTE treatment/secondary prevention. Criteria require documented diagnosis and typically confirmation of indication. Dabigatran also funded for hip/knee arthroplasty VTE prophylaxis.
entries.nzEquivalent: Same drugs - SA pathway. Warfarin remains funded without restriction. PHARMAC has specific SA forms for each DOAC and indication.

entries.originStatus: Low-dose codeine combinations (8 mg) were available OTC in Australia and the UK until recent rescheduling. Panadeine Forte (30 mg codeine) was prescription-only. The UK upscheduled all codeine combinations to prescription-only in 2010. Australia followed with stricter controls from 2018.
entries.nzStatusNote: All codeine-containing products require a prescription in NZ. There are no OTC codeine products. Patients from Australia or the UK may be surprised by this. Paracetamol-codeine combinations (e.g. Panadeine equivalent) are available on prescription.
entries.nzEquivalent: Paracetamol (OTC, funded). Ibuprofen (OTC). For moderate pain: paracetamol + codeine by prescription, or tramadol (prescription). NSAIDs by prescription for higher-dose needs.

entries.originStatus: Available OTC in the US (Aleve, 220 mg) and the UK (250 mg) for pain and dysmenorrhoea.
entries.nzStatusNote: Naproxen is prescription-only in NZ at all doses. Patients from the US or UK may be accustomed to buying it at the pharmacy without a prescription.
entries.nzEquivalent: Ibuprofen 200-400 mg (OTC). Diclofenac (prescription). Naproxen by prescription if preferred.

entries.originStatus: Available in most countries with dermatologist oversight. PBS-funded in Australia. In many countries, GPs with appropriate training can prescribe.
entries.nzStatusNote: Isotretinoin (Oratane in NZ) is funded via Special Authority, requiring initiation by a dermatologist. GPs can continue prescribing once the SA is established. Pregnancy prevention programme requirements apply.
entries.nzEquivalent: Same drug (Oratane). Requires dermatology referral for initiation. Continuation prescriptions can be written by the GP.

entries.originStatus: Various testosterone gel formulations are funded or widely available overseas. In Australia, Androfeme (testosterone cream for women) is registered and used for female sexual dysfunction.
entries.nzStatusNote: Testosterone is funded in NZ for males with confirmed hypogonadism (SA required). Testosterone for female sexual dysfunction: Androfeme is not registered in NZ. Androgel/Testogel - some formulations available by private prescription. Topical testosterone for women is sometimes prescribed off-label from compounded preparations.
entries.nzEquivalent: For males: testosterone undecanoate injection (Reandron) funded via SA. Testosterone gel formulations - discuss with endocrinology. For females: no registered product; compounding may be required.

entries.originStatus: Very commonly prescribed in the US (Ambien) for insomnia. Available in Australia (Stilnox) by prescription.
entries.nzStatusNote: Zolpidem is funded in NZ but classed as a controlled drug (Class C). Prescribing is subject to standard controlled drug requirements. It is not first-line - CBT-I and sleep hygiene are recommended first. PHARMAC funds it but many practitioners use it cautiously given dependence and next-day impairment risk.
entries.nzEquivalent: Same drug, available but controlled. Melatonin (Circadin) funded via SA for age ≥55. CBT-I preferred non-pharmacological approach.

entries.originStatus: Funded on PBS in Australia for T2DM. Available in the US and UK. Considered highly effective for both T2DM and weight management.
entries.nzStatusNote: Tirzepatide is not currently funded in NZ (as of mid-2026). It is not listed on the PHARMAC schedule. Available by private prescription only. PHARMAC funding applications may be in progress - check current status.
entries.nzEquivalent: Semaglutide (Ozempic) via SA for T2DM. For weight loss: orlistat via SA. No funded equivalent with comparable efficacy.

entries.originStatus: Prescription-only in the UK (8 mg codeine OTC was formerly allowed but restricted). In the US, Tylenol with Codeine (including T3, T4) is commonly prescribed.
entries.nzStatusNote: Codeine/paracetamol combinations are available in NZ by prescription. The brand names differ. Patients from the UK may be on paracetamol 500 mg / codeine 30 mg - the NZ equivalent is Panadeine with codeine 30 mg, by prescription.
entries.nzEquivalent: Paracetamol + codeine 30 mg by prescription. Tramadol as an alternative if stronger analgesia needed.

entries.originStatus: Both IR and XR formulations widely funded and used internationally for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and (controversially) augmentation of depression.
entries.nzStatusLabel: IR funded; XR via Special Authority
entries.nzStatusNote: Quetiapine immediate-release (IR) is funded in NZ. The extended-release (XR) formulation requires Special Authority. Patients on XR overseas may need to switch to IR or obtain SA documentation.
entries.nzEquivalent: Quetiapine IR (funded, no SA required). XR available via SA - initiation typically requires psychiatric input.

entries.originStatus: First-line for uncomplicated UTI in UK NICE guidelines and many US guidelines. Widely funded internationally.
entries.nzStatusNote: Nitrofurantoin is funded in NZ and used for uncomplicated lower UTI. However, it should be avoided if eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m² due to reduced efficacy and potential toxicity. Patients with renal impairment arriving on nitrofurantoin from the UK may need an alternative.
entries.nzEquivalent: Trimethoprim (funded, first-line in NZ for uncomplicated UTI). Nitrofurantoin available if trimethoprim-resistant. Note: local resistance patterns matter - check HealthPathways.
